draft.introduction: Romans 13:1–14 addresses the Christian’s relationship to governing authorities, declaring that “there is no authority except from God.” This passage has shaped political theology from Constantine through the Reformation.

translation.text: Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment.

translator_notes.note: This passage has been used to justify political quietism and obedience to tyrants, but must be read alongside Acts 5:29 (“we must obey God rather than men”) and Revelation 13, where the state becomes a beast. Paul writes during a period of relative peace under Nero’s early reign.
